title: Enteral feeding in the clinically stable newborn
executiveSummary: Initiate and advance enteral feeding in clinically stable newborns weighing 500 g to 2500 g (days 0–30), prioritizing maternal milk, utilizing a progressive volume based on weight and tolerance, and maintaining surveillance for signs of NEC or aspiration.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Confirm clinical stability: no mechanical ventilation, no vasoactive drugs, soft abdomen, present peristalsis, and meconium passage completed or in progress.
2. Assess the feeding route: direct breastfeeding if GA ≥ 34 weeks with established suction-swallowing-respiration coordination; orogastric tube (preferred) or nasogastric tube if preterm or presenting with weak suction.
3. Prioritize the mother's own milk; if unavailable, use donor milk from a certified milk bank; infant formula should only be used if there is a contraindication to breast milk, as prescribed.
4. Initiate feeding based on weight and GA (see table); use a syringe pump or slow gavage (≥ 10–15 min) in newborns < 1500 g.
5. Position the newborn in right lateral recumbency or semi-Fowler at 30° during gavage feeding; do not infuse while the newborn is flat supine.
6. Verify gastric residual volume before each feeding in newborns < 1500 g or those at high risk: aspirate gently; if it is < 50% of the feeding volume and non-bilious, return it and proceed with feeding; if it is bilious, voluminous, or accompanied by abdominal distension, hold the feed and notify.
7. Advance volume by 10–20 ml/kg/day if tolerance is adequate: absence of vomiting, distension, bloody stools, or feeding-related apneas.
8. Record every feeding: volume, route, type of milk, gastric residual volume, vomiting, stools, and daily weight.
9. Promote breastfeeding: expression every 2–3 hours, skin-to-skin contact, and family support; human milk fortifier should only be added if indicated by the neonatologist.
10. Supplement with vitamin D (400 IU/day from the first days of life if exclusively breastfed) and iron (2–4 mg/kg/day starting at week 4 in preterm infants) according to protocol.
11. In case of feeding intolerance: maintain minimum trophic feeding (10–20 ml/kg/day) if possible; evaluate for NEC, sepsis, or intestinal obstruction.
12. Transition to exclusive breastfeeding or bottle-feeding once effective suction is achieved and weight gain is ≥ 15–20 g/day.

## Scientific rationale

### Population and scope

**Newborns day 0–30**, **500 g–2500 g**, **clinically stable** without enteral contraindications. Newborns with severe distress, NEC, intestinal obstruction, or hemodynamic instability require parenteral nutrition and an intensive care protocol.

### Indications

- Stable newborn with a mature or progressively maturing gastrointestinal tract.
- Transition from parenteral to enteral nutrition in intermediate/conventional care units.
- Nutritional maintenance during rooming-in.

### Contraindications

- Confirmed or suspected NEC (abdominal distension, blood in stools, pneumatosis).
- Intestinal obstruction or uncorrected atresia.
- Shock or hemodynamic instability.
- Recurrent apneas/bradycardias triggered by feeding.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Source:** WHO — Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 6 months; initiation within the first hour of life reduces neonatal mortality (WHO, 2024).

> **Source:** VON — Early trophic feeding in stable VLBW infants improves intestinal maturation without increasing the incidence of NEC, provided advancement is careful (Vermont Oxford Network).

### Initial volume and advancement

| Weight / GA | Initial volume | Advancement | Frequency |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 500–749 g | 10–20 ml/kg/day (trophic) | 10–15 ml/kg/day | Every 3 hours |
| 750–999 g | 20–40 ml/kg/day | 15–20 ml/kg/day | Every 3 hours |
| 1000–1499 g | 40–60 ml/kg/day | 20 ml/kg/day | Every 3 hours |
| 1500–2500 g | 60–80 ml/kg/day | 20 ml/kg/day | Every 3 hours or ad libitum breastfeeding |

**Target:** 150–180 ml/kg/day by days 7–14, depending on tolerance.

### Tube selection

| Preference | Rationale |
| --- | --- |
| Orogastric | Lower risk of aspiration and nasal injury in preterm infants |
| Nasogastric | Only if OG is not viable; secure and verify position |

Verify position before every gavage feed: aspiration of gastric contents or air auscultation (less reliable).

### Criteria for feeding intolerance — hold feed and notify

- Gastric residual volume > 50% of the feeding volume on two consecutive occasions.
- Bilious or bloody vomiting.
- Abdominal distension, periumbilical erythema, or bloody stools.
- Apnea/bradycardia during or after feeding.

### Expected weight gain

- Term newborn: 15–30 g/day following physiological weight loss (days 3–5).
- Preterm infant: 10–20 g/day/kg once full feeds are established.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Suspected NEC (abdominal distension, blood in stools, lethargy, thrombocytopenia).
- Aspiration (coughing, cyanosis, desaturation during gavage feeding).
- Dehydration or hypoglycemia due to prolonged fasting.
- Failure to gain weight for > 3 days despite full enteral feeding.

bibliography.citation: World Health Organization. Infant and young child feeding — early initiation of breastfeeding.
Vermont Oxford Network. Nutrition and feeding protocols for very low birth weight infants.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Breastfeeding and the use of human milk — policy statement.
World Health Organization. Guidelines on optimal feeding of low birth weight infants in low- and middle-income countries.
